chapters.title: Unit 1 Let's be Friends The Best of Friends
Unit 3 Together We Live The Three Little Rabbits
Unit 4 A Big Surprise Five Friendly Farmers
Unit 5 The Food We Eat Junk Food, Junk Food-Go Away...
Unit 6 The Witty Nasruddin The Clever Jackal
Unit 7 I Was Bad at Cricket Our Legacy
Unit 8 Will Power We Shall Overcome

topics.name: Bridging Gap Activity Picture - 1
topics.summary: Students observe a picture depicting a family engaged in various daily activities and answer questions to describe the scene, people, and their actions.

topics.name: Bridging Gap Activity Picture - 2
topics.summary: Students observe a picture depicting a village scene with people interacting and answer questions for description and conversation practice.

topics.name: Bridging Gap Activity Picture - 3
topics.summary: Students observe a picture depicting a bustling market scene and use it as a basis for producing descriptions, conversations, and stories.

topics.name: Bridging Gap Activity Picture - 4
topics.summary: Students observe a picture depicting a street scene with children playing and adults working, and use it for description and conversation practice.

topics.name: UNIT 1 Let's Be Friends
topics.summary: This topic introduces the unit theme of friendship and the main character, Hari, through an initial image and discussion questions about his behavior.

topics.name: A. Reading
topics.summary: This topic presents the story "Let's Be Friends" about a boy named Hari who is disliked for his habit of pinching others. He learns a lesson when he is pinched by a crab during a picnic.

topics.name: Glossary
topics.summary: This topic provides definitions for key vocabulary words encountered in the story "Let's Be Friends".

topics.name: Comprehension
topics.summary: This topic includes exercises designed to assess students' understanding of the story "Let's Be Friends" through open-ended and multiple-choice questions.

topics.name: Vocabulary
topics.summary: This topic features a crossword puzzle where students identify various food items based on descriptive clues.

topics.name: Grammar
topics.summary: This topic introduces the concept of articles (a, an, the), explains their usage rules, and provides examples from the story for practice.

topics.name: Grammar: Articles and Adverbs
topics.summary: Students practice using articles in a letter and learn about adverbs, then apply them in fill-in-the-blank exercises. They also practice using the correct form of words.

topics.name: Writing: Notice
topics.summary: Students learn about writing notices by examining an example of Do's and Don'ts for the seashore, then practice writing a notice for school surroundings.

topics.name: Writing: Personal Reflection
topics.summary: Students engage in a writing activity to express their thoughts and feelings about an event, fostering self-expression and reflection.

topics.name: Writing: Dialogue and Role-play
topics.summary: Students complete a dialogue between two characters and are encouraged to role-play it, developing their conversational writing and oral communication skills.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing: Punctuation
topics.summary: Students learn and practice using capital letters, full stops, commas, and question marks correctly in sentences.

topics.name: Editing Written Work
topics.summary: Students are introduced to a checklist to help them improve their written descriptions, conversations, and stories by self-editing for grammar, punctuation, and sequencing.

topics.name: Enrich your English: Describing People
topics.summary: Students learn vocabulary to describe people's physical attributes and clothing, enhancing their descriptive writing skills.

topics.name: B. Reading: The Best of Friends (Poem)
topics.summary: Students read and recite the poem "The Best of Friends," which emphasizes the value of friendship and mutual support.

topics.name: Glossary (for "The Best of Friends")
topics.summary: Students learn the meanings of key vocabulary words from the poem "The Best of Friends" to aid comprehension.

topics.name: Comprehension (for "The Best of Friends")
topics.summary: Students answer questions and express their agreement with statements related to the poem "The Best of Friends" to demonstrate their understanding.

topics.name: Writing: Personal Narrative/Reflection
topics.summary: Students engage in personal writing activities, reflecting on their friendships and narrating incidents of helping or being helped by friends.

topics.name: Our Feelings / Emotional Literacy
topics.summary: Students learn about the importance of understanding and sharing their feelings, such as happiness, sadness, anger, and fear, with friends who offer acceptance and support.

topics.name: Unit Self-Assessment
topics.summary: Students evaluate their understanding of the unit's content and skills through a self-assessment checklist, covering listening, reading, speaking, and writing activities.

topics.name: Introduction to Karate Kitten
topics.summary: Students are introduced to the chapter theme through a picture of a pet shop and initial questions, followed by instructions for a listening activity about Rohit and his pet.

topics.name: The Story "Karate Kitten"
topics.summary: Students read the story of "Karate Kitten" about a family adopting a mischievous kitten, with embedded comprehension questions to guide their understanding.

topics.name: Vocabulary and Comprehension Exercises
topics.summary: Students learn new vocabulary words from the story and complete comprehension exercises, including true/false statements and answering questions, along with a vocabulary building activity.

topics.name: Grammar - Prepositions
topics.summary: Students learn about prepositions, their function, and practice using them in various contexts.

topics.name: Grammar - Sentence Types
topics.summary: Students learn about the definition of a sentence, and different types of sentences including declarative, interrogative, imperative, and exclamatory sentences. They practice identifying and forming these sentence types.

topics.name: Grammar - Combining Sentences with 'When'
topics.summary: Students learn how to combine two past actions using the conjunction 'when' to show that one action was complete while another was ongoing, or that two actions were complete.

topics.name: Writing Activities
topics.summary: Students engage in various writing activities, including describing a pet, writing about pet care, and completing a dialogue for a role-play.

topics.name: Poem - Our Kittens
topics.summary: Students read and comprehend the poem "Our Kittens," learn new vocabulary, identify rhyming words, and understand similes.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing & Pangrams
topics.summary: Students learn about proper punctuation usage and are introduced to pangrams as sentences containing all letters of the alphabet.

topics.name: Project Work
topics.summary: Students engage in a group project to collect information about pets from their friends, formulating questions and recording data in a table.

topics.name: Fun time
topics.summary: Students read a short humorous story about a pet shop and a faithful puppy, followed by an instruction to share a joke with the class.

topics.name: How well did I understand this unit?
topics.summary: A self-assessment activity where students evaluate their understanding and mastery of various skills and concepts covered throughout Unit 2.

topics.name: Together We Live
topics.summary: Introduces Unit 3 with a picture of a village scene, prompting students to observe and discuss, followed by a listening comprehension activity.

topics.name: A. Reading - Together We Live
topics.summary: The main story of Unit 3, detailing the villagers' journey from a drought-stricken village to a new town and their efforts to find food and shelter through community effort.

topics.name: Glossary and Comprehension
topics.summary: Introduces new vocabulary from the story and assesses students' understanding of the narrative through questions and a true/false activity.

topics.name: Vocabulary
topics.summary: Focuses on expanding vocabulary through identifying objects, writing sentences, and forming compound words.

topics.name: Collocations
topics.summary: Introduces the concept of collocations (words that often go together) and provides exercises for identifying and using them in sentences.

topics.name: Grammar
topics.summary: Focuses on grammar concepts including choosing suitable adjectives to complete sentences and using correct forms of words (verbs/nouns) in sentences.

topics.name: Writing
topics.summary: Students practice writing stories based on a sequence of pictures and writing sequential steps for preparing food items. They also engage in oral discussion about feelings related to a story.

topics.name: B. Reading - The Three Little Rabbits
topics.summary: Students read a play script about three little rabbits who build their own houses and face a big bad fox, learning about cooperation and problem-solving.

topics.name: Glossary
topics.summary: Students learn new vocabulary words and their meanings from the story "The Three Little Rabbits".

topics.name: Enact the play in the class
topics.summary: Students are instructed to perform the play "The Three Little Rabbits" in class, encouraging dramatic expression and teamwork.

topics.name: Writing - Opinion on the play
topics.summary: Students write their opinions about the play "The Three Little Rabbits", considering various aspects like characters, memorable moments, and personal preferences.

topics.name: QR Code Activity
topics.summary: Students engage with digital content linked via a QR code, likely related to the chapter's themes or activities.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing
topics.summary: Students learn and practice using capital letters and various punctuation marks (full stop, comma, question mark, exclamation mark) correctly in sentences.

topics.name: Enrich your English - Idioms
topics.summary: Students learn common English idioms and their meanings to enhance their vocabulary and understanding of figurative language.

topics.name: Fun Time
topics.summary: This section presents a short dialogue between two rabbits discussing how to react if a tiger appears. It encourages creative thinking and oral communication by asking students to share a joke with the class.

topics.name: How well did I understand this unit?
topics.summary: This topic provides a self-assessment checklist for students to evaluate their understanding and mastery of various skills and concepts covered in the unit, including listening, reading comprehension, oral communication, vocabulary (compound words, adjectives), and writing (recipe steps, story, opinion).

topics.name: A Big Surprise - Introduction
topics.summary: Students are introduced to the unit's theme through a poster analysis and a listening activity about a girl named Lalitha and her tomato seeds.

topics.name: A. Reading - A Big Surprise Story
topics.summary: Students read a story about Lalitha, a ten-year-old girl who grows a large tomato, plans to enter it in an exhibition, and experiences a surprise when it goes missing.

topics.name: Glossary
topics.summary: Students learn new vocabulary words from the story "A Big Surprise" with their definitions.

topics.name: Comprehension of "A Big Surprise" Story
topics.summary: Students answer questions and identify key events to demonstrate their understanding of the story "A Big Surprise".

topics.name: Vocabulary - Word Maze
topics.summary: Students practice identifying and recognizing names of fruits and vegetables within a word maze.

topics.name: Grammar - Past Tense Verbs
topics.summary: Students learn about the simple past tense, how to form it for regular and irregular verbs, and practice using past tense verbs in sentences.

topics.name: Vocabulary - Pulses and Vegetables Classification
topics.summary: Students classify a list of food items into 'Pulses (-gram)' or 'Vegetables (-gourd)' categories based on their common suffixes.

topics.name: Grammar - Auxiliary Verbs
topics.summary: Students learn to identify and understand the function of auxiliary (helping) verbs in sentences.

topics.name: Writing - Letter Writing
topics.summary: Students learn to read an informal letter and practice writing a response letter.

topics.name: Writing - Dialogue and Role-play
topics.summary: Students practice writing a conversation between two characters and then role-playing it.

topics.name: Poster Analysis and Creation
topics.summary: Students analyze information presented in posters and then create their own poster based on a given theme.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing
topics.summary: Students learn and practice using capital letters, full stops, commas, exclamation marks, and inverted commas in sentences.

topics.name: Enrich your English - Qualities of a Student
topics.summary: Students explore how each letter of the word 'student' can represent a positive quality, and then apply this concept to describe their own qualities.

topics.name: Five Friendly Farmers - Poem
topics.summary: Students read and recite a poem about five friendly farmers and their daily chores.

topics.name: Glossary for "Five Friendly Farmers"
topics.summary: Students learn the definitions of new vocabulary words from the poem 'Five Friendly Farmers'.

topics.name: Comprehension of "Five Friendly Farmers"
topics.summary: Students answer questions based on the poem 'Five Friendly Farmers', identify farmer activities, and understand word meanings.

topics.name: Group Work - Crossword Clues
topics.summary: Students work in groups to create clues for a given crossword puzzle.

topics.name: Fun Time
topics.summary: Students read a short joke about being late for school due to a road sign and are encouraged to share jokes with their friends.

topics.name: How well did I understand this unit?
topics.summary: Students self-assess their understanding and completion of various learning objectives and activities from the unit by ticking 'Yes', 'Somewhat', or 'No' in a provided checklist.

topics.name: Introduction to Food and Health
topics.summary: Introduces the unit theme of food and health through a picture, notice board, and initial discussion questions.

topics.name: A. Reading: The Food We Eat
topics.summary: A reading passage about the importance of good food, balanced diet, and the dangers of junk food, presented as a talk by Dr. Aruna.

topics.name: Glossary: Vocabulary related to Food and Health
topics.summary: Introduces key vocabulary words from the reading passage with their definitions.

topics.name: Comprehension Exercises
topics.summary: Exercises to test students' understanding of the reading passage.

topics.name: Vocabulary Application and Classification
topics.summary: Exercises focused on classifying food items and using vocabulary in context through fill-in-the-blanks.

topics.name: Vocabulary Game: Stepping Stones
topics.summary: A word game where students fill in blanks on stepping stones based on clues, often antonyms or descriptions.

topics.name: Grammar: Short Forms and Sentence Connectors
topics.summary: This topic introduces students to common short forms of words and teaches them how to combine sentences using the connectors 'because' and 'though'.

topics.name: Writing: Advertisement Analysis and Poster Creation
topics.summary: This topic focuses on developing writing skills through analyzing an advertisement and creating a poster for an event.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing and Social Expressions
topics.summary: This topic teaches students about proper punctuation in writing and introduces common English expressions for various social situations.

topics.name: Poem: Junk Food- Junk Food – Go Away...
topics.summary: This topic features a poem about the negative aspects of junk food, encouraging healthy eating habits. It includes reading, comprehension, and group activities.

topics.name: Project Work and Unit Self-Assessment
topics.summary: This topic involves a group project comparing homemade food and junk food, followed by a self-assessment to gauge understanding of the unit's concepts.

topics.name: The Witty Nasruddin (Comic Strip)
topics.summary: Students read a comic strip about Gopal and his neighbour, introducing the concept of a pot having babies, leading to a humorous situation.

topics.name: A. Reading - The Witty Nasruddin
topics.summary: Students read a story about a poor traveller, an eatinghouse, and the clever Hodja (Nasruddin) who outsmarts the eatinghouse keeper.

topics.name: Glossary and Comprehension
topics.summary: Students learn new vocabulary from the story and answer comprehension questions, including sequencing events and multiple-choice questions.

topics.name: Vocabulary - Homophones
topics.summary: Students learn about homophones, words that sound alike but have different spellings and meanings, and practice identifying and using them in sentences.

topics.name: Pronouns
topics.summary: Students practice using appropriate pronouns (e.g., They/we, They/it, he/you, He/she, they/them, our/ours, your/yours, my/mine, their/theirs) in sentences.

topics.name: Grammar - Adjectives - Degrees of Comparison
topics.summary: Students learn about the three degrees of comparison for adjectives: positive, comparative, and superlative, how to form them, and practice using them in sentences and tables.

topics.name: Writing
topics.summary: Students learn about writing notices, read an example, answer questions, and practice writing their own notice.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing
topics.summary: Students learn about using capital letters, full stops, commas, and inverted commas in sentences.

topics.name: Enrich your English
topics.summary: Students expand their vocabulary by learning words describing emotions and qualities.

topics.name: B. Reading The Clever Jackal
topics.summary: Students read a comic strip story about a clever jackal who outsmarts a tiger.

topics.name: Comprehension
topics.summary: Students answer questions and summarize the story "The Clever Jackal" to demonstrate their understanding.

topics.name: III. Complete the following table.
topics.summary: Students identify who spoke certain lines and to whom in "The Clever Jackal" story.

topics.name: Project Work
topics.summary: Students engage in a self-assessment activity to evaluate their understanding of the unit's various topics and skills.

topics.name: Unit Introduction and Pre-reading Activities
topics.summary: Introduction to Unit 7 with a photograph-based discussion and listening comprehension questions related to cricket.

topics.name: Reading: I Was Bad at Cricket
topics.summary: A story about a boy named Somla who was not good at cricket, his experience with an injury, and his eventual success.

topics.name: Comprehension and Vocabulary
topics.summary: Exercises to test understanding of the story "I Was Bad at Cricket" and to build vocabulary.

topics.name: Grammar: Modals
topics.summary: Introduction to modal verbs, focusing on 'must' for obligation/determination and 'could' for possibility/request/permission, with practice exercises.

topics.name: Writing
topics.summary: This topic focuses on developing writing skills through imaginative and descriptive tasks related to personal experiences and story characters.

topics.name: Project Work
topics.summary: This topic introduces the concept of multiple intelligences and encourages students to identify their own skills and those of their family members, promoting self-awareness and appreciation for diverse abilities.

topics.name: Crossword Puzzle
topics.summary: This topic involves completing a crossword puzzle using provided pictures as clues, enhancing vocabulary and problem-solving skills.

topics.name: Reading: Our Legacy
topics.summary: This topic introduces students to a poem titled "Our Legacy," encouraging reading comprehension and appreciation for poetic language.

topics.name: Vocabulary, Comprehension, and Prefixes
topics.summary: This topic expands vocabulary related to the poem, tests comprehension of its themes, and introduces common English prefixes and their meanings.

topics.name: Fun Time and Unit Self-Assessment
topics.summary: This topic includes a light-hearted activity for oral communication and a self-assessment checklist for students to evaluate their understanding and learning from the entire unit.

topics.name: Will Power
topics.summary: This topic introduces the concept of will power and overcoming challenges through a photograph and a listening activity about disability.

topics.name: A. Reading - Will Power
topics.summary: Students read a biographical text about the life and achievements of Stephen William Hawking, focusing on his resilience and will power despite his physical challenges.

topics.name: Glossary
topics.summary: Students learn the definitions of key vocabulary words from the reading passage about Stephen William Hawking.

topics.name: Comprehension
topics.summary: Students answer questions, identify true/false statements, choose correct options, and complete a table based on the reading passage about Stephen William Hawking.

topics.name: Vocabulary
topics.summary: Students practice identifying synonyms, singular/plural forms, and antonyms of words, enhancing their vocabulary skills.

topics.name: Grammar - Gender of Nouns
topics.summary: Students learn about the four types of gender for nouns: masculine, feminine, common, and neuter, with examples for each.

topics.name: Writing
topics.summary: Students read a speech delivered by Lalitha about Stephen Hawking and answer comprehension questions related to the speech's content and structure.

topics.name: Work in groups.
topics.summary: Students work in groups to select a great personality, collect information, prepare a speech, plan its structure, discuss and correct it, write it on a chart, and present it to the class.

topics.name: Conventions of Writing
topics.summary: Students learn and practice the correct use of capital letters, full stops, commas, and inverted commas in sentences.

topics.name: Enrich your English
topics.summary: Students practice pronunciation and fluency by reciting tongue twisters.

topics.name: B. Reading
topics.summary: Students read and sing the inspirational song "We shall Overcome".

topics.name: Project Work
topics.summary: Students engage in group work to identify and understand the strengths and challenges of children with special needs, culminating in a presentation.

topics.name: How well did I understand this unit?
topics.summary: Students self-assess their understanding of various topics and activities covered in the unit.

topics.name: Unit 1 Let's Be Friends
topics.summary: This topic presents a short story about children at school, focusing on an incident where one child pinches another, leading to a discussion among friends and the teacher's arrival.

topics.name: Unit 2 Karate Kitten
topics.summary: This topic presents a dialogue between two friends, Kiran and Rohit, discussing an injury Rohit's father sustained due to a new pet kitten.

topics.name: Unit 3 Together We Live
topics.summary: This topic presents a story about a family facing severe water scarcity and crop failure, highlighting their struggles and decision to seek help from the gram panchayat.

topics.name: Unit 4 A Big Surprise
topics.summary: This topic presents a dialogue between a girl, Lalitha, and her father about planting various vegetable seeds in their garden, focusing on the process of growing plants.

topics.name: Unit 5 The Food We Eat
topics.summary: This topic presents a dialogue between a mother, Dr. Aruna, and her children about the importance of eating a healthy breakfast before school.

topics.name: Unit 6 The Witty Nasruddin
topics.summary: This topic presents three short humorous anecdotes featuring Nasruddin, showcasing his wit and clever responses.

topics.name: Unit 7 I Was Bad at Cricket
topics.summary: This topic presents a first-person narrative about a cricket match where the narrator, initially feeling unprepared, performs exceptionally well and leads their team to victory.

topics.name: Unit 8 Will Power
topics.summary: This topic presents a dialogue between Rahul and his father, where Rahul is upset about being teased for his disability, and his father encourages him by sharing the story of Stephen Hawking.

topics.name: About the Textbook
topics.summary: This topic introduces the textbook, its academic standards, broad categories of academic standards, types of discourses, and the two types of assessments (Formative and Summative). It also details the criteria for awarding marks for observation, notebooks, projects, and slip tests, and the evidence for awarding marks.

topics.name: Learning Outcomes
topics.summary: This topic lists the specific learning outcomes for the learner in English, detailing various skills such as acting according to instructions, reciting poems, answering questions, conducting interviews, using correct sentences, reading stories, writing simple letters, attempting creative writing, appreciating verbal/written content, and using different kinds of words.

topics.name: General Service List - Common Words of English
topics.summary: This topic provides a list of common English words, numbered from 401 to 700, intended to expand the learner's vocabulary.

topics.name: Textbook Development Team - 2013
topics.summary: This topic lists the individuals and teams involved in the development and publication of the textbook in 2013, including advisors, editors, contributors, illustrators, layout designer, and the QR Code team.

topics.name: NOTES
topics.summary: A blank lined page provided for students to write notes.

subtopics.name: Observation and Description of a Family Scene
subtopics.summary: Students practice observing details in a picture and describing the setting, objects, people, and their activities.

subtopics.name: Observation and Description of a Village Scene
subtopics.summary: Students practice observing details in a picture and describing the setting, objects, people, and their activities in a village context.

subtopics.name: Conversation Practice based on Picture
subtopics.summary: Students engage in conversation practice by identifying characters, predicting dialogue, and formulating responses based on the visual cues in the picture.

subtopics.name: Picture-based Description, Conversation, and Story Creation
subtopics.summary: Students use a detailed market scene picture to practice describing elements, engaging in conversations, and developing short stories.

subtopics.name: Picture-based Description and Conversation
subtopics.summary: Students use a detailed street scene picture to practice describing elements and engaging in conversations.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Hari and his actions
subtopics.summary: Students observe an image depicting Hari pinching another boy and discuss the situation and their feelings about it.

subtopics.name: Introduction of Hari's character
subtopics.summary: The story introduces Hari, a boy who enjoys pinching and playing tricks, leading to him being disliked by other children.

subtopics.name: Picnic and Hari's isolation
subtopics.summary: The class goes for a picnic, and Hari finds himself isolated and alone, eating his lunch by himself.

subtopics.name: Hari's encounter with the crab
subtopics.summary: Hari encounters a monster crab, attempts to shake its claw, and gets pinched, experiencing pain similar to what he inflicts on others.

subtopics.name: Hari's realization and change of heart
subtopics.summary: Hari reflects on his actions after being hurt by the crab and realizes how his behavior has affected others, resolving to change.

subtopics.name: Word definitions
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meanings of words such as 'black and blue', 'bruise', 'hoarse', 'isolated', 'pincer', 'stalk', 'nipped', and 'yelled'.

subtopics.name: Open-ended comprehension questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions that require them to recall details, infer feelings, and provide opinions about the story.

subtopics.name: Multiple-choice comprehension questions
subtopics.summary: Students select the correct answer from given options to demonstrate their understanding of specific events and motivations in the story.

subtopics.name: Food items crossword puzzle
subtopics.summary: Students use clues to fill in a crossword grid with the names of different food items.

subtopics.name: Introduction to articles
subtopics.summary: Students are reminded of articles 'a', 'an', and 'the' as grammatical components.

subtopics.name: Rules for using 'a', 'an', and 'the'
subtopics.summary: Students learn the specific rules for using 'a' before consonant sounds, 'an' before vowel sounds, and 'the' for specific or already mentioned nouns.

subtopics.name: Examples of article usage
subtopics.summary: Students analyze examples from the story to understand the application of article rules in context.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Adverbs
subtopics.summary: Students learn that words like 'quietly', 'hard', and 'fast' are adverbs because they modify verbs, explaining how actions took place.

subtopics.name: Notice about Seashore Do's and Don'ts
subtopics.summary: An example notice providing guidelines for behavior at the seashore, categorized into 'Do's' and 'Don'ts'.

subtopics.name: Expressing Thoughts
subtopics.summary: Students are asked to write down their thoughts about an incident, encouraging personal reflection and written expression.

subtopics.name: Completing a Dialogue
subtopics.summary: Students complete a conversation between Hari and Raghu, practicing dialogue writing and preparing for role-play.

subtopics.name: Using Punctuation Marks
subtopics.summary: Instructions on using capital letters, full stops, commas, and question marks to correctly punctuate sentences.

subtopics.name: Self-Editing Checklist
subtopics.summary: A checklist with questions to guide students in reviewing and improving their written work, focusing on sentence structure, punctuation, sequencing, and spelling.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary for Describing People
subtopics.summary: A table providing descriptive words for various aspects of a person's appearance, including height, build, age, face, hair, eyes, and clothes.

subtopics.name: The Best of Friends Poem
subtopics.summary: The poem "The Best of Friends" by S. Jill Wolf, highlighting the qualities and importance of true friendship.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary from Poem
subtopics.summary: Definitions for words like 'frown', 'trials', 'worth', and 'secret' as used in the poem.

subtopics.name: Answering Questions about the Poem
subtopics.summary: Students answer direct and interpretive questions based on the content of "The Best of Friends" poem.

subtopics.name: Expressing Agreement
subtopics.summary: Students evaluate statements related to the poem and indicate their agreement, providing reasons for their choices.

subtopics.name: Describing a Best Friend
subtopics.summary: Students write about their best friend and explain why that person is their best friend.

subtopics.name: Narrating an Incident
subtopics.summary: Students write a short narrative about an experience of helping a friend or receiving help from someone.

subtopics.name: Understanding and Sharing Feelings
subtopics.summary: A text explaining that people have many feelings and that friends are important for sharing emotions and accepting each other's strengths and weaknesses.

subtopics.name: Unit Understanding Checklist
subtopics.summary: A checklist for students to assess how well they understood various aspects of the unit, including listening texts, reading passages, discussions, exercises, poems, and writing tasks.

subtopics.name: Picture Observation and Discussion
subtopics.summary: Students observe a picture of a pet shop and discuss related questions.

subtopics.name: Listening Comprehension Introduction
subtopics.summary: Students are instructed to listen to their teacher narrate a story about Rohit and his pet, then answer questions based on the narration.

subtopics.name: Reading the Story
subtopics.summary: Students read the story "Karate Kitten" which describes Rohit's family, their visit to a pet shop, and the kitten's antics.

subtopics.name: Embedded Comprehension Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions embedded within the story text to check their understanding of the plot and characters.

subtopics.name: Glossary
subtopics.summary: Students learn definitions for key vocabulary words from the "Karate Kitten" story.

subtopics.name: Comprehension - Tick and Rewrite
subtopics.summary: Students evaluate statements related to the story, ticking correct ones and rewriting incorrect ones.

subtopics.name: Comprehension - Answer Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer detailed questions based on the "Karate Kitten" story.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary - Frame Meaningful Phrases
subtopics.summary: Students combine words from two boxes (pets and features) to create descriptive phrases.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Prepositions
subtopics.summary: Students observe underlined words in sentences to identify prepositions and learn their definition and common examples.

subtopics.name: Practice with Prepositions
subtopics.summary: Students complete passages and sentences by filling in appropriate prepositions or prepositional phrases.

subtopics.name: Definition of a Sentence
subtopics.summary: Defines a sentence as a group of words making complete sense, starting with a capital letter and ending with a full stop, exclamation, or question mark.

subtopics.name: Imperative and Exclamatory Sentences
subtopics.summary: Introduces and defines imperative sentences (expressing advice, request, command, suggestion) and exclamatory sentences (expressing strong or sudden feeling).

subtopics.name: Combining Sentences with 'When'
subtopics.summary: Explains how to combine sentences describing two past actions using 'when'.

subtopics.name: Describing a Pet
subtopics.summary: Students practice descriptive writing by continuing a description of the Karate Kitten and then describing their own chosen pet.

subtopics.name: Completing a Dialogue
subtopics.summary: Students complete a conversation between Janaki, Rohit, and a shopkeeper at a pet shop, preparing for a role-play.

subtopics.name: Reading the Poem "Our Kittens"
subtopics.summary: Students read the poem "Our Kittens" by Evaleen Stein.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary from the Poem
subtopics.summary: Students learn new words from the poem such as 'cunning', 'paw', 'claws', and 'gay' through a glossary.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Similes
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to similes as a form of comparison, with examples provided.

subtopics.name: Punctuation Rules
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the correct use of capital letters, full stops, commas, question marks, exclamation marks, and inverted commas.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Pangrams
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to pangrams as sentences that contain every letter of the English alphabet.

subtopics.name: Collecting Information about Pets
subtopics.summary: Students learn to formulate questions and gather data about pet animals and birds from their friends, practicing group work and information organization.

subtopics.name: Reading a Humorous Story and Sharing Jokes
subtopics.summary: Students read a short story with a humorous twist and are encouraged to tell a joke to the class, promoting oral communication and enjoyment of language.

subtopics.name: Unit Self-Assessment Checklist
subtopics.summary: Students reflect on their learning progress in listening, reading, comprehension, grammar (prepositions, combining sentences), poetry recitation, and writing skills related to the unit's content.

subtopics.name: Picture Analysis and Discussion
subtopics.summary: Students observe a picture depicting villagers and their environment, then answer questions based on their observations.

subtopics.name: Listening Comprehension - True or False
subtopics.summary: Students listen to a narrative related to the picture and determine whether given statements are true or false.

subtopics.name: Story Reading
subtopics.summary: Students read the story 'Together We Live' about villagers migrating due to drought and finding a solution in a new town.

subtopics.name: Glossary
subtopics.summary: Students learn new words and their meanings from the story 'Together We Live', including 'reluctantly', 'caked', 'lush', 'prosperous', 'ganji', 'fetched', and 'creaked'.

subtopics.name: Story Comprehension Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions to demonstrate their understanding of the story's plot, characters' feelings, and themes.

subtopics.name: Identifying and Describing Objects
subtopics.summary: Students identify items carried by villagers in the story and write sentences about them.

subtopics.name: Compound Words
subtopics.summary: Students learn about compound words and practice forming them by combining words from two given lists.

subtopics.name: Understanding Collocations
subtopics.summary: Students learn what collocations are through examples like 'a dash of salt and pepper' and other expressions using 'a dash of'.

subtopics.name: Practicing Collocations and Sentence Framing
subtopics.summary: Students identify words that collocate from a given list and then use these collocations to form their own sentences.

subtopics.name: Adjectives
subtopics.summary: Students practice choosing appropriate adjectives from a given list to complete sentences correctly.

subtopics.name: Correct Word Forms
subtopics.summary: Students practice using the correct grammatical forms of words (e.g., converting nouns to adjectives or verbs to nouns) provided in brackets to complete sentences.

subtopics.name: Story Writing from Pictures
subtopics.summary: Students write a story by observing a series of pictures.

subtopics.name: Oral Discussion on Feelings
subtopics.summary: Students discuss and present their feelings about a given scenario in groups.

subtopics.name: Sequential Writing - Recipes
subtopics.summary: Students write down the steps involved in preparing food items like Ganji and Payasam.

subtopics.name: Play Reading - Scene 1
subtopics.summary: Students read the first scene of the play where the three little rabbits decide to build their own houses.

subtopics.name: Song/Poem - "I'm off to build a beautiful new house"
subtopics.summary: Students read a short song or poem about building a new house.

subtopics.name: Play Reading - Scene 2
subtopics.summary: Students read the second scene where the rabbits build houses of hay, sticks, and bricks, and the first two rabbits encounter the fox.

subtopics.name: Song/Poem - "I've built a beautiful new house"
subtopics.summary: Students read a short song or poem about having built a new house.

subtopics.name: Play Reading - Scene 3
subtopics.summary: Students read the third scene where the big bad fox tries to blow down the first rabbit's house of hay.

subtopics.name: Play Reading - Scene 4
subtopics.summary: Students read the fourth scene where the big bad fox tries to blow down the second rabbit's house of sticks.

subtopics.name: Play Reading - Scene 5
subtopics.summary: Students read the fifth scene where the big bad fox tries to blow down the third rabbit's house of bricks, and the rabbits work together to outsmart the fox.

subtopics.name: Song/Poem - "Ha, ha, ha, ho, ho, ho"
subtopics.summary: Students read a short song or poem expressing joy and not being afraid of the fox.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary - Idioms
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meaning of the idiom "huff and puff".

subtopics.name: Vocabulary - Adjectives
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meaning of the adjective "blazing".

subtopics.name: Play Performance
subtopics.summary: Students perform the play they have read, taking on different roles.

subtopics.name: Expressing Opinion in Writing
subtopics.summary: Students formulate and write their personal opinions about a literary piece, guided by specific prompts.

subtopics.name: Digital Content Access
subtopics.summary: Students use a QR code to access supplementary digital learning materials.

subtopics.name: Punctuation and Capitalization
subtopics.summary: Students apply rules for capital letters and punctuation marks to rewrite sentences correctly.

subtopics.name: Understanding Idioms
subtopics.summary: Students read and comprehend the meanings of various English idioms.

subtopics.name: Dialogue: Two Rabbits and a Tiger
subtopics.summary: Students read a short dialogue where two rabbits discuss different strategies for dealing with a tiger encounter, one suggesting running and the other suggesting standing still like a doll.

subtopics.name: Unit Self-Assessment Indicators
subtopics.summary: A list of indicators covering listening comprehension, reading comprehension of "Together We Live" and "The Three Little Rabbits", discussing feelings, understanding compound words and adjectives, and writing skills (recipe steps, story, opinion on the play).

subtopics.name: Poster Analysis
subtopics.summary: Students analyze a poster for an "Agri based products Exhibition" and answer questions about its content.

subtopics.name: Listening Comprehension
subtopics.summary: Students listen to a story about Lalitha (from Appendix-I) and answer questions related to her father's gardening and her desire to grow tomato seeds.

subtopics.name: Story Narrative
subtopics.summary: The story details Lalitha's gardening efforts, her sister Kavitha's help, the growth of the tomato, its disappearance, and its eventual reappearance at an exhibition where Lalitha wins a prize.

subtopics.name: Word Definitions
subtopics.summary: A list of words like 'grow', 'weed', 'insect', 'worm', 'pour', 'blossomed', 'tiny', 'at once', 'raised', 'pretty', 'weeping', 'display' are defined.

subtopics.name: Answering Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer direct questions about the plot, characters, and themes of the story.

subtopics.name: Identifying Main Actions
subtopics.summary: Students identify and list the main actions or events that occur in the story.

subtopics.name: Identifying Fruits and Vegetables
subtopics.summary: Students locate and circle five names of fruits and vegetables hidden in a grid.

subtopics.name: Understanding Past Tense
subtopics.summary: Explanation of how '-ed' is added to regular verbs for past tense, and examples of irregular verbs and verbs that do not change form.

subtopics.name: Forming Past Tense
subtopics.summary: Students complete sentences by filling in the blanks with the correct past tense form of given verbs.

subtopics.name: Categorizing Food Items
subtopics.summary: Students sort given words into two categories based on whether they are pulses (ending in -gram) or vegetables (ending in -gourd).

subtopics.name: Identifying Auxiliary Verbs
subtopics.summary: Explanation of auxiliary verbs (has, am, do, did, be forms) and their role in supporting main verbs or acting as main verbs in certain contexts.

subtopics.name: Underlining Auxiliaries
subtopics.summary: Students read sentences and underline the auxiliary verbs.

subtopics.name: Reading a letter
subtopics.summary: Students read a letter from Lalitha to her friend Usha about winning a prize for growing a big tomato.

subtopics.name: Writing and role-playing a conversation
subtopics.summary: Students write a dialogue between Lalitha and her father about growing tomato plants and then act it out.

subtopics.name: Analyzing posters
subtopics.summary: Students examine two posters, one about an Agri based products Exhibition and another about a Kids Wear Exhibition Cum Sale, to extract specific information and complete a table.

subtopics.name: Creating a poster
subtopics.summary: Students work in groups to design and prepare a poster for 'Environment Day Celebrations' including necessary information.

subtopics.name: Punctuation and capitalization
subtopics.summary: Students apply rules of capitalization and punctuation to rewrite a given sentence correctly.

subtopics.name: Observing qualities from a word
subtopics.summary: Students observe how each letter in the word 'student' is used to describe a quality (Sincere, Truthful, Unique, Decent, Efficient, Neat, Talented).

subtopics.name: Reading and reciting the poem
subtopics.summary: Students read and recite the poem 'Five Friendly Farmers' which describes the activities of farmers.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary definitions
subtopics.summary: Definitions are provided for words like 'chore', 'plough', 'pen', and 'ripe' as used in the poem.

subtopics.name: Answering comprehension questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer direct questions about the poem and identify activities related to farmers, as well as choose the correct meaning of a word from the poem.

subtopics.name: Creating crossword clues
subtopics.summary: Students analyze a crossword grid with pre-filled words and collaboratively write appropriate clues for each word, both 'Across' and 'Down'.

subtopics.name: Reading a Joke
subtopics.summary: Students read a short dialogue between Mrs. Malathi and Rajesh, which forms a joke.

subtopics.name: Telling Jokes
subtopics.summary: Students are prompted to tell jokes to their friends.

subtopics.name: Unit Self-Assessment
subtopics.summary: Students reflect on their learning and engagement with the unit's content and exercises.

subtopics.name: Observing and Discussing the Unit Theme
subtopics.summary: Students observe a picture of children looking at a notice board about 'A Young Mind in a Sound Body' and discuss related questions.

subtopics.name: Understanding Good Food and Balanced Diet
subtopics.summary: Students read about why food is vital, qualities of good food, and examples of healthy meals.

subtopics.name: Understanding Unhealthy Food and Junk Food
subtopics.summary: Students read about junk food, additives, preservatives, and the influence of advertisements on food choices.

subtopics.name: Learning New Vocabulary
subtopics.summary: Students learn definitions for words like vital, survival, nutritious, fragrant, mouth-watering, junk food, pester, additive, and preservative.

subtopics.name: Answering Comprehension Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer direct and inferential questions based on 'The Food We Eat' text.

subtopics.name: Identifying Speakers
subtopics.summary: Students identify whether children or Dr. Aruna spoke certain expressions from the text.

subtopics.name: Classifying Food Items
subtopics.summary: Students identify junk food from a given list and provide reasons.

subtopics.name: Completing a Paragraph with Vocabulary
subtopics.summary: Students fill in blanks in a story about a food festival using provided vocabulary words.

subtopics.name: Replacing Underlined Words
subtopics.summary: Students replace underlined words in a story with more suitable words from a given box.

subtopics.name: Playing a Vocabulary Game
subtopics.summary: Students play a game to practice vocabulary by filling in blanks with words that match given clues.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Short Forms
subtopics.summary: Students learn that words like 'Dr.' and 'TV' are short forms for 'doctor' and 'television' respectively.

subtopics.name: Sentence Connector 'because'
subtopics.summary: Students learn to combine two sentences using the word 'because' to show cause and effect.

subtopics.name: Sentence Connector 'though'
subtopics.summary: Students learn to combine two sentences using the word 'though' to express a contrast or concession.

subtopics.name: Analyzing an Advertisement
subtopics.summary: Students read and understand the details presented in a newspaper advertisement.

subtopics.name: Poster Creation
subtopics.summary: Students learn to design a poster for an event, including essential details like venue, date, time, and prizes.

subtopics.name: Punctuation Rules
subtopics.summary: Students learn to correctly use capital letters, full stops, commas, question marks, and inverted commas.

subtopics.name: Enrich your English: Messages for different occasions
subtopics.summary: Students learn appropriate phrases and messages for occasions like birthdays, wishing someone well, congratulating, consoling, thanking, apologizing, and asking for help.

subtopics.name: Reading and Reciting the Poem
subtopics.summary: Students read and recite the poem "Junk Food- Junk Food – Go Away..." which advocates against consuming junk food.

subtopics.name: Glossary for the Poem
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meaning of new words found in the poem, such as 'wanna' and 'thee'.

subtopics.name: Project Work: Homemade Food vs. Junk Food
subtopics.summary: Students work in groups to discuss and present points for and against homemade food versus junk food.

subtopics.name: Unit Self-Assessment
subtopics.summary: Students evaluate their own understanding of the unit's content through a checklist.

subtopics.name: Comic Strip Story
subtopics.summary: Students read a multi-panel comic strip depicting a humorous story about Gopal borrowing and returning pots, claiming they had babies.

subtopics.name: Story: The Witty Nasruddin
subtopics.summary: Students read the story of a poor traveller who enjoys the smell of food from an eatinghouse, and how Hodja (Nasruddin) cleverly pays for the 'smell' of the food.

subtopics.name: Glossary
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meanings of key words from the story: traveller, customer, magistrate, pennies, journey.

subtopics.name: Comprehension Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions, arrange sentences in sequence, and select the correct option to demonstrate understanding of the story 'The Witty Nasruddin'.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Homophones
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to homophones using the example of 'two' and 'to', explaining that they sound the same but have different spellings and meanings.

subtopics.name: Identifying and Using Homophones
subtopics.summary: Students identify homophones from the story and use them to construct new sentences.

subtopics.name: Using Pronouns
subtopics.summary: Students choose the correct pronoun from given options to complete sentences.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Degrees of Comparison
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of adjectives with examples and rules for forming them (adding -er, -est).

subtopics.name: Practicing Degrees of Comparison
subtopics.summary: Students complete tables and fill in blanks with the correct forms of adjectives in their positive, comparative, or superlative degrees.

subtopics.name: Reading a Notice Board
subtopics.summary: Students read an example of a notice board with instructions for an eating house.

subtopics.name: Use capital letters, full stops (.), commas (,) and inverted commas (“ ”) wherever necessary and rewrite the following sentences.
subtopics.summary: Instructions on using punctuation and capitalization.

subtopics.name: 1.Words describing emotions
subtopics.summary: A list of emotion words and their synonyms (e.g., happy - joyous, delighted, glad, cheerful, merry).

subtopics.name: 2. We have many qualities. Different people have different qualities. Here is a list of a few qualities. Circle the ones you think you have. Think of more qualities you or your friends and family members have. Add them to the list.
subtopics.summary: A list of quality words (e.g., adventurous, aggressive, bold) for students to identify with and expand upon.

subtopics.name: The Clever Jackal
subtopics.summary: The comic strip illustrates a story where a farmer traps a tiger, the tiger asks for help, and a jackal cleverly helps the farmer.

subtopics.name: How well did I understand this unit? Read and tick (✓) in the appropriate box.
subtopics.summary: A checklist for students to self-evaluate their learning in the unit, covering listening, reading, character discussion, vocabulary (homophones), grammar (degrees of comparison), and writing (notice, story).

subtopics.name: Glossary
subtopics.summary: Definitions of key vocabulary words from the story, including 'groaned', 'butter fingers', 'screamed', and 'consoled'.

subtopics.name: Modals: Must (Obligation, Duty, Determination)
subtopics.summary: Explanation of the modal verb 'must' and its use to express necessity, obligation, duty, and determination, with examples from the story.

subtopics.name: Modals: Could (Possibility, Request, Permission)
subtopics.summary: Explanation of the modal verb 'could' and its use to express possibility, polite requests, and permission, along with a list of other common modal verbs.

subtopics.name: Writing about Somla's thoughts
subtopics.summary: Students write about the thoughts of a character, Somla, after surgery, focusing on cricket.

subtopics.name: Writing about a favourite game
subtopics.summary: Students write a descriptive piece about their favourite game, including details like its name, reasons for liking it, duration of playing, frequency, and memorable experiences.

subtopics.name: Skill Diary: Group discussion on skills
subtopics.summary: Students work in groups to list different skills among their classmates and understand that skills are abilities to do things well.

subtopics.name: Multiple Intelligences
subtopics.summary: Students learn about nine different types of intelligences and identify which ones are well-developed in themselves and their family members.

subtopics.name: Picture-based crossword
subtopics.summary: Students solve a crossword puzzle by identifying words represented by pictures, filling in both 'Across' and 'Down' sections.

subtopics.name: Reading the poem "Our Legacy"
subtopics.summary: Students read a poem about teamwork, courage, determination, and the importance of moving forward from past mistakes to achieve victory and preserve a positive legacy.

subtopics.name: Glossary
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meanings of key vocabulary words from the poem, such as 'critical', 'determine', 'grit', 'excel', 'obvious', 'fury', 'legacy', and 'preserve'.

subtopics.name: Comprehension Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions based on the poem "Our Legacy" to demonstrate their understanding of its message about responsibility, determination, and justice.

subtopics.name: Enrich your English: Prefixes
subtopics.summary: Students learn the meanings of common English prefixes like 'pre-', 'over-', 'mis-', 'inter-', 'dis-', 'co-', 're-', and 'under-'.

subtopics.name: Fun Time: Joke Telling
subtopics.summary: Students engage in an oral activity where they take turns telling jokes to the class, based on a provided example dialogue.

subtopics.name: Unit Self-Assessment
subtopics.summary: Students reflect on their learning throughout the unit by ticking boxes to indicate their level of understanding and completion of various activities, including listening, reading, speaking, puzzles, grammar, and writing.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Will Power
subtopics.summary: Students observe a photograph of an archer with a disability and discuss questions related to determination and difference.

subtopics.name: Listening Activity: Story of Rahul
subtopics.summary: Students listen to a story about a school boy named Rahul who overcomes his disability and answer related questions.

subtopics.name: Biography of Stephen William Hawking
subtopics.summary: Students read about Stephen William Hawking's early life, education, the onset of his illness, his scientific contributions, and how he overcame his disability through determination.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary Definitions
subtopics.summary: Students are provided with definitions for words such as 'hailed', 'sclerosis', 'reputation', 'rely', and 'honorary degree'.

subtopics.name: Answering Comprehension Questions
subtopics.summary: Students answer direct questions about the life and achievements of Stephen William Hawking based on the reading text.

subtopics.name: True or False Statements
subtopics.summary: Students evaluate statements about Stephen William Hawking's life and correct the false ones.

subtopics.name: Multiple Choice Comprehension
subtopics.summary: Students select the correct option to complete sentences related to Stephen William Hawking's life and challenges.

subtopics.name: Information Extraction Table
subtopics.summary: Students extract specific dates and events from the reading text to complete a chronological table about Stephen William Hawking's life.

subtopics.name: Synonyms
subtopics.summary: Students choose the word nearest in meaning to an underlined word in given sentences.

subtopics.name: Singular/Plural Forms
subtopics.summary: Students find the singular or plural forms of given words using a dictionary.

subtopics.name: Antonyms
subtopics.summary: Students find words from the text that are opposite in meaning to a given list of words.

subtopics.name: Masculine Gender Nouns
subtopics.summary: Students learn that words for male animals, boys, and men are called masculine gender nouns, with examples like 'son', 'king', 'brother'.

subtopics.name: Feminine Gender Nouns
subtopics.summary: Students learn that words for female animals, girls, and women are called feminine gender nouns, with examples like 'daughter', 'queen', 'sister'.

subtopics.name: Common Gender Nouns
subtopics.summary: Students learn that words for both males and females are called common gender nouns, with examples like 'child', 'doctor', 'cousin'.

subtopics.name: Neuter Gender Nouns
subtopics.summary: Students learn that things without life are said to be of neuter gender, with examples like 'table', 'fan', 'car'.

subtopics.name: Analyzing a Speech
subtopics.summary: Students read Lalitha's speech on 'The Lives of Great People', which focuses on Stephen Hawking, and answer questions about its introduction, main points, and conclusion.

subtopics.name: Speech Preparation
subtopics.summary: Students learn the steps involved in preparing and presenting a speech about a great personality.

subtopics.name: Punctuation and Capitalization
subtopics.summary: Rules for using capital letters, full stop, comma, and inverted commas.

subtopics.name: Tongue Twisters
subtopics.summary: A collection of tongue twisters to improve speaking fluency.

subtopics.name: We shall Overcome
subtopics.summary: Lyrics of the song "We shall Overcome" by Martin Luther King.

subtopics.name: Understanding the Song
subtopics.summary: Questions to check comprehension of the themes and messages in "We shall Overcome".

subtopics.name: Group work on Special Needs Children
subtopics.summary: Guidelines for group research and presentation on children with special needs.

subtopics.name: Unit Self-Assessment
subtopics.summary: A checklist for students to evaluate their learning and comprehension of the unit's content.

subtopics.name: Let's Be Friends Story
subtopics.summary: A narrative text describing an incident at school involving Raghu and Hari, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: Karate Kitten Dialogue
subtopics.summary: A dialogue text between Kiran and Rohit about an incident involving a kitten, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: Together We Live Story
subtopics.summary: A narrative text about a family's experience with drought and water shortage, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: A Big Surprise Dialogue
subtopics.summary: A dialogue text between Lalitha and her father about gardening and planting seeds, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: The Food We Eat Dialogue
subtopics.summary: A dialogue text about breakfast and healthy eating habits, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: Nasruddin's Jokes
subtopics.summary: Three short humorous stories about Nasruddin, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: Cricket Story
subtopics.summary: A first-person narrative text about a cricket game, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: Will Power Dialogue
subtopics.summary: A dialogue text between Rahul and his father about overcoming challenges and disability, intended for listening practice.

subtopics.name: Academic Standards
subtopics.summary: Describes the six broad categories of Academic Standards expected for children to achieve by the end of the academic year: Listening and Responding, Reading Comprehension, Conventions of Writing, Vocabulary, Grammar, and Creative Expression.

subtopics.name: Discourses
subtopics.summary: Lists the ten types of discourses children should listen to, read, and construct, both orally and in written form: Descriptions, Conversations, Stories/Narrative, Rhymes/songs, Diary, Slogans, Posters/notice/invitation, Letter, and Essay.

subtopics.name: Assessment
subtopics.summary: Explains that the textbook contains eight theme-based units with listening passages and reading texts, along with textual exercises covering vocabulary, grammar, writing, and project work. It also introduces the two types of assessments: Formative assessment and Summative assessment.

subtopics.name: Formative assessment
subtopics.summary: Details that formative assessment is done four times a year based on four tools: Observation, Notebooks, Project work, and Slip tests. It specifies the marks allotted for each and describes the cumulative nature of assessment.

subtopics.name: Criteria for awarding marks under each tool
subtopics.summary: Provides detailed criteria for awarding marks for Observation, Notebooks, Projects, and Slip Tests, outlining what aspects of children's performance and work are considered.

subtopics.name: Summative Assessment
subtopics.summary: Explains that summative assessments are conducted twice a year, covering all academic standards, with marks allotted for oral and written tests.

subtopics.name: The learner....
subtopics.summary: A comprehensive list of expected learning outcomes for the student, covering various aspects of English language proficiency.

subtopics.name: 401 to 700 Words (Part 1)
subtopics.summary: A list of common English words, numbered from 401 to 544, presented in a multi-column format.

subtopics.name: 401 to 700 Words (Part 2)
subtopics.summary: A continuation of the list of common English words, numbered from 545 to 700, presented in a multi-column format.

subtopics.name: Advisors, Editors, Contributors
subtopics.summary: Lists the names and affiliations of the advisors, editors, and contributors who worked on the textbook development.

subtopics.name: Illustrators, Layout Designer, Textbook Publication Team, QR Code Team
subtopics.summary: Lists the names of the illustrators, layout designer, the members of the Textbook Publication Team, and the QR Code Team.

subtopics.name: Blank Lined Pages for Notes
subtopics.summary: Students are provided with blank lined pages to record information, practice writing, or make personal notes.

exercises.text: 1. What is the place you see in the picture? 2. What things do you see in the picture? 3. Who are the people you see in the picture? 4. What are they doing?
Interactive questions for description: 1. What is the place you see in the picture? 2. What things do you see in the picture? 3. Who are the people you see in the picture? 4. What are they doing?

Interactive questions for conversation: 1. Name the characters speaking. (two characters in conversation) 2. Who would speak first? 3. What would the first character say? 4. What would be the second character’s response?
Note : Use this picture for producing description, conversation and story.
Note : Use this picture for producing description and conversation.

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] Note: The box provides for providing instructions and comments.
Look at the picture and answer the following questions. 1. Why do you think Hari is pinching Raghu? 2. Suppose you were in Raghu's place, would you allow that?

Now, your teacher will tell you about Hari. Listen to him/ her carefully and answer the following questions. (Note : Listening text is in Appendix-1) 1. What do you think of Hari pinching Raghu? 2. What do you think the children would tell the teacher? 3. What do Hari's classmates think about him?

I. Answer the following questions. 1. Did Hari have friends? Why/ Why not? 2. If you were left alone by your classmates, how would you feel? 3. How did the seaside creatures teach Hari a lesson? 4. Do you think Hari has changed? Give reason for your opinion.

II. Answer the following questions by putting a tick (✓) on the right answers. 1. All the children left Hari alone because a. he troubled others. b. he liked to be alone. c. the teacher asked them to do so. 2. Hari went and sat in a sandy corner because a. he was happy to be alone. b. he liked that place. c. he was left alone by his friends. 3. The monster crab put out his hand to a. shake hands. b. pull Hari into the pool. c. take his lunch.

Hari and his classmates took some food items to the picnic. Complete the grid using the clues given below to get the names of the food items. Down 1. I am a fruit. I am round and juicy. 3. I am a three-lettered word. You eat me with bread. 5. Children like me very much. The last four letters are l-a-t-e. Across 2. I am a fruit. You eat me by peeling my skin. 4. I am cold, sweet and creamy. Children like me very much. 6. I am spongy and soft. You cut and eat me on your birthday. 7. I am an eight-lettered word. If you drop the last four letters from my name, I will be in the desert. 8. I am a five-lettered word. If you drop the first letter and the fourth letter from my name, it will be the name of a colour. 9. I am a fruit. Eat me every day to keep healthy. 10. I am a three-lettered word and I have a yellow ball in my stomach.
1. Pick out more examples of 'a/an and the' from the story.

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] UNIT 1

a. Hattie put her hand to shake the child's hand.
b. Hattie put her hand to shake the child's class.
c. Hattie put her hand to shake the child's head.

In sentence: "Hattie put her hand to shake the child's head," we use "come before singular nouns beginning with consonant letters."
In sentence: "Hattie put her hand to shake the child's class," we use "come before singular nouns beginning with consonant letters."

Dear Teja, I am fine, and hope to hear the same from you. Pupils of our class went for ______ picnic to ______ seaside last week. I was left alone. So, I went to ______ sandy corner and sat down. My mother packed me ______ egg, ______ cake and ______ chocolate. At that time, ______ large sandy lobster crawled out of ______ pool. All the sea animals tried to shake hands with me. But, they pricked me till I was black and blue. They taught me ______ lesson, not to hurt anyone. Hari.

Read and understand the following idioms and their meanings. 1. A hot potato : A disputed issue spoken by many people. 2. Beat around the bush : Avoiding the main topic or not speaking directly about the issue. 3. Cry over spilt milk : When you complain about the about the loss from the past. 4. Last straw : The final problem in a series of problems. 5. Hit the nail on the head : Do or say something exactly right. 6. Ball is in our court : It is upto you to make the next decision. 7. Let the cat out of the bag : To share the information that was previously concealed.

The simple past tense is used to show the actions happened in the past. We add "ed" to some verbs to form the simple past tense. Some verbs take an object and add "ed" to it to form the simple past tense. For example, the simple past tense of the verb "eat" is "I ate." We also use the simple past tense to form the past tense of the verb "give" and "take."

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] 1. Read the following sentences.
1. "Someone has stolen my beautiful tomato!"
2. "I am going to the zoo tomorrow."
3. "Why don't you take your big tomato to the exhibition?"
4. "I am not going to the exhibition."
The words has, am, do, did in these sentences are not main verbs. They are auxiliary verbs. Auxiliary verbs are used to show the action of the main verb. They are also called helping verbs. The main verb is followed by the auxiliary verb and its other forms (ideas, did and done) appear only in sentences as main verbs.
Examples: You are a beautiful woman.
Rajesh is a boy.
I am a teacher.
She does something useful everyday.
Radha has done her homework.
I am a singer.
They were very hungry.

The Food We Eat
"Food is vital for our survival. From the time that we are babies, we need good food to grow strong. Good food has many qualities. Can you tell what are they? Write them down.
Some children answered, "It is natural." Others said, "It is nutritious."
"Kinds," said Dr. Anna. "Balanced food is good because it provides body with the necessary nutrients. It also helps to build strong bones and teeth. Good food has all children need for healthy life. Do you think children should eat food that is safe for us to eat?" Dr. Anna continued, "Good food is truly healthy because it has the flavour of fresh fruits and vegetables. It is also good for our heart, brain, and bones. It is also good for our skin. We should eat healthy food that is made for us (by manufacturers or what we bring from home).
"Yes," said the children. "We should eat healthy food."

One day, Lalitaha participated in a seminar. Good news! She was selected to speak at the seminar. The speaker, Dr. L. M. Haskins, was talking about the life of Stephen Hawking.
"Do you know who Stephen Hawking is?" asked the audience.
Dr. L. M. Haskins, who is known as one of the greatest scientists of the world, was recovering from a serious illness. He had been in a wheelchair for the past 10 years.
Of course, his achievements may seem remarkable to us. What I want to tell you is that he was a person who was very humble. He was a person who had a lot of respect for himself. He had a lot of knowledge. He had a lot of wisdom. He had a lot of integrity. He had a lot of remarkable contributions to the field of science even though he couldn't walk. He had a lot of determination. He had a lot of compassion.
Unskatingly, Lalitaha has life a source of inspiration to everyone. He is a person who has made a significant contribution to the world. He is a person who has a lot of respect for himself. He is a person who has a lot of integrity. He is a person who has a lot of wisdom. He is a person who has a lot of compassion.
Answer the following questions.
1. How did Lalitaha feel about her speech?
2. Which are the points she stressed and how are they organized? 3. How did she conclude her speech?

Write your speech on a chart.

sidebars.text: Note to the teacher:- The pictures given for bridging activities are meant for classroom interaction in order to equip the children to acquire basic language competencies and familiarise with the primary discourses i.e., description, conversation and story writing. The transaction process suggested is a whole class, group and individual activity. These pictures can be used for writing descriptions, conversations and stories. The teacher can also make use of some more pictures which have scope for interaction. The teacher may refer the teacher’s handbook for detailed process.
QR code: J2W6A1
QR code: U3X1T1
QR code: A3V8G8
QR code: D313S5

c. he liked the sand
d. he liked to sit there by his friends
3. The monster crab put out his hand to
a. Hari
b. Hari into the pond
c. Hari's hand
d. Hari's foot

Sometimes, apart from hitting or pinching, you may hear comments made on the body and about your looks. Remember, we did not make our body. We need not take pride in or feel ashamed of our body. Pride and shame come from behaviour. When others tease or bully you, it tells you about the kind of person the bully is. The blame is theirs. They need to feel ashamed of their behaviour - not you.
QR code: 16J1Y5

large group of children crawled out of the water. The sea animals tried to shake hands with me. But they pricked my skin. I was not able to swim. I was very scared. I was not to hurt anyone.
Hari

[IMAGE] Our Feelings
We have many feelings. We generally share them with others. We express our feelings through words like happiness, sadness, anger, surprise, confusion, etc. We also express our feelings through actions like dancing, singing, playing, etc. We accept our strengths and weaknesses equally. They understand our feelings and try to help us. We also accept the feelings of others.

[IMAGE] A. Reading
Karate Kitten
When Robi was six, his family lived in a small town. His father Rajendra was a teacher at the local company. Robi was a very keen homemaker. She used to be alone in the day time when Robi was at school. One day, Robi was playing with his cat named Janaki. The cat was very keen to be a pet. Robi's father saw this and asked his mother, "Why did you allow your cat to play with Robi?" The mother replied, "Robi was in a pet shop at that time." The young shopkeeper advised Janaki to take Robi to the shop. The shopkeeper was very happy. He gave Robi a new toy.
• Why did Janaki go to the pet shop to get a new toy?
• Why did Robi ask his mother, "Why did you allow your cat to play with Robi?"
• "Because it's special."
• "What is special about the kitten?" Janaki asked.

[IMAGE] A. Reading
A Big Surprise
Labiba, a ten-year-old girl, sowed some tomato seeds in the middle of the field. The tomatoes were growing very well. One day, she saw a small insect in the plants. She pulled every weed and never allowed an insect in a wolftail-like plants. When the insect was in the plants, she picked it and threw it away. The plants were planted in all the corners of her plot and she kept it beautiful. The flowers also grew in the plants. This year, she planted many plants. The flowers were grown and the tiny tomatoes were growing to show. Labiba and her friend were very happy. The plants were watered the plants daily. A very beautiful large tomato grew on the plant.

Now it's time to work on the shapes and make a list of the different skills that girls and boys in your class have. Skills are what we are able to do well.
To do anything well, we need multiple intelligences.
Multiple Intelligences
Multiple Intelligences are the different kinds of intelligence. All intelligences are equally valuable and present in every individual. Girls and boys in your class have different kinds of intelligence. We can develop multiple intelligences in our children by doing different activities.
Tick the intelligences that are well developed in you. Ask your family members to tick the ones that are well developed at them. In there a multiple intelligences.
Multiple Intelligences

2. Good with words and writing. Writing poems, stories.
3. Good with music and art. Making music. Arranging my room.
4. Good with danger problems.
5. Good with physical, singing, playing musical instruments.
6. Good with friends and family.
7. Feeling good about myself.
8. Good with God. Thinking about life and its purpose.

Will Power
Exactly three years after the death of the great scientist Galileo, Stephen William Hawking was born on 14th January, 1942. Little did his parents know that their son would become one of the most prominent scientists of the history.
After schooling, Stephen joined University College, Oxford and studied Physics. He was awarded a degree in 1964 and later a Ph.D. in 1967. He went to the University of Cambridge to do research. Since 1970, he has held Harpel of professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford.
After age of 17, Stephen started teaching physics. He was a teacher of physics for a long time and later became a type of scientist, which weakened his health. He was also a famous person in the world. He predicted an early death for him.
In 1979, Stephen was awarded the Nobel Prize for his contributions to physics. He carried on his research. He was not
able to walk.

[IMAGE] Writing
One day, Lalita participated in a seminar. Good day, everyone, she said. She was wearing a black dress.
Respected sir, teachers and fellow students! I am very happy to be here today. I am going to talk about the life of Stephen Hawking.
Do you know who Stephen Hawking is?
He is one of the greatest scientists of the world. He recognized history's darkest periods and made it into the most important periods in human history.
Of course, his achievements may seem negligible to us. What I want to tell you is that he was a person who was very intelligent. He was so intelligent that he could solve any problem in a few minutes. He had such a great knowledge of science, he had made remarkable contributions to the field of science even though he couldn't communicate well.
Unsurprisingly, Lalita had life a source of inspiration to everyone. He was a person who was very intelligent. He was a person who believed through will power, one can achieve wonders in one's life.
The text also provides information about the topic.
Answer the following questions.
1. How did Lalita start her speech?
2. Which are the points she stressed and how are they organized? 3. How did she conclude her speech?
